turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has our opinion in case 16-240, Weaver versus Massachusetts.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: During a criminal trial on an unjustified courtroom closure occurs, and there is an objection from defense counsel, and that issue has been raised on direct review, this Court labels the error structural. Put in concrete terms, that means that the error entitles a defendant to a new trial automatically. In this case, the courtroom was closed to the public for two days of jury selection during petitioner’s murder trial. Defense counsel did not object to the closure, however, nor did petitioner raise the issue on direct appeal. Instead, petitioner first raised the courtroom closure claim in a motion for a new trial five years later. He raised the claim and arguing that his attorney had provided ineffective assistance by failing to object to the closure. Now, when defendant attempts to obtain a new trial on the basis of ineffective assistance here, she must show two things. First, the defendant must show that the attorney was deficient. In this case comes to the Court on the assumption that the petitioner has made that showing that counsel was deficient. Second, the defendant must show prejudice. To do so in most cases, the defendant must show that, but for counsel’s error, there was a reasonable probability that the jury would have returned a different verdict. That showing has not been made here. There is no reason to think that the jury would not have convicted petitioner if only his attorney had objected to the courtroom closure. Even so, petitioner argues that he can show prejudice in a different way, namely by showing that the attorney’s errors led to a fundamentally unfair trial. Assuming petitioner is correct that prejudice can be shown in this manner, there is no reason to think that petitioner’s trial was fundamentally unfair. The trial was not conducted in secret or in a remote place; closure was limited to the jury voir dire; the courtroom remained open during the evidentiary phase of the trial. The closure decision apparently was made by court officers rather than the judge. Many members of the venire panel, who did not become jurors, did observe the proceedings, and there was record of the proceeding, and the record indicates no basis for concern other than the closure itself. There has been no showing furthermore that the potential harms flowing from a courtroom closure came to pass in this case. For example, there is no suggestion that any jury lied during voir dire, no suggestion of misbehavior by the prosecutor, judge or any other party, no suggestion that any of the participants failed to approach their duties with the neutrality and serious purpose that our system demands. In the criminal justice system, the constant and indeed the unending duty of the judiciary is to seek and define a proper balance. The balance is between the necessity for a fair and just trial and the importance for finality of judgments. When a structural error is preserved and raised on direct review, the balance is in the defendant’s favor, and a new trial generally will be granted as a matter of right. When structural error is raised in the context of an ineffective-assistance claim, however, finality concerns are far more pronounced. For this reason and in light of the other circumstances present in this case, petitioner must show prejudice in order to obtain a new trial, he has not made the required showing. The judgment of the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court is affirmed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Gorsuch has joined. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which Justice Gorsuch has joined. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kagan has joined.
